---
title: "Conclusion"
---

This project examined how Schwartz’s basic human values relate to political attitudes in Europe between 2018 and 2024, with a focus on the COVID-19 pandemic as a potential turning point. The findings show that value structures remain remarkably stable over time and across generations. Conservation values are consistently higher among older cohorts, while younger generations score lower, and this hierarchy does not change across ESS rounds. Similarly, Universalism remains a stable predictor of support for income redistribution across all generations, although the strength of this relationship is modest.

At the same time, the pandemic period is associated with clear but limited shifts. Political trust declines significantly from the pre-pandemic period and does not fully recover by 2024, suggesting a more durable trust erosion. Conservation values show only a temporary and partial decline during the pandemic, indicating that large-scale shocks do not fundamentally reshape value priorities but may temporarily weaken them.

A key limitation of this study is that causal interpretation is restricted by the cross-sectional nature of most comparisons and potential measurement differences across countries and rounds. Additionally, aggregation at generational and regional levels may conceal important within-group heterogeneity. Future research could extend this analysis using longitudinal individual-level panel data and further explore how institutional context moderates value–attitude links.

Overall, the results suggest a dual pattern of stability and disruption: core human values remain structurally stable, while political trust and some value–attitude relationships are more sensitive to societal shocks such as the COVID-19 pandemic.
